coredata.dc:title: Collagen derived carbon quantum dots for cell imaging in 3D scaffolds via two-photon spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Dehghani, Alireza
Ardekani, Sara Madadi
Hassan, Mahbub
Gomes, Vincent G.
coredata.dc:description: Biocompatible and photostable fluorescent probes are crucial yet challenging to develop for visualising and tracking biological functions and interactions that occur in living organisms. We synthesised biocompatible CQDs with 15% QY and tested them for cellular and scaffold imaging at variable depths. The CQDs, synthesised from collagen under controlled and benign conditions in a hydrothermal reactor, were characterised for their key physicochemical properties. The fluorescence characteristics were determined using two-photon microscopy, and based on our results we propose a mechanism for CQD luminescence by combining the carbogenic core and edge-effect contributions to the PL behaviour. The bioimaging of cells embedded in a luminescent 3D printed scaffold showed that CQDs enable imaging at depths of about 1500 μm under biomimetic conditions. Real-time videography and imaging tests showed differential visualisation of individual cells and scaffold. The excellent photostability and non-photobleaching characteristics of CQDs make them suitable for long-term whole cell and tissue imaging via multi-photon microscopy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Collagen
Quantum dot
Scaffold
Bioimaging
Multiphoton confocal microscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell culture and cell viability experiments
Luminescent scaffold 3D printing and cell seeding

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge financial support through Australian Postgraduate Award ( SC0042 ) (authors AD and SMA) and assistance from Dr D. Lai and Dr. S. Hua for the biology-related work. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of CQD synthesis, scaffold construction and multi-photon imaging. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) TEM images of CQDs. Inset shows FFT of nanoparticles with no visible diffraction (scale: 20 nm), (b) Particle size distribution of CQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS spectra of CQDs: (a) Full survey spectrum, (b) C1s spectrum, (c) N1s spectrum, and (d) O1s spectrum. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical properties of CQDs: (a) UV-Vis absorption spectra in aqueous dispersion, (b) PL spectra with variable excitation wavelengths, (c) Effect of pH on emission intensities and (d) Lifetime intensity decay profile. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Up-converted emission of CQDs excited at different excitation wavelengths. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Two photon imaging using a 780 nm pulsed laser: (a) Cyan represents nuclei stained with DAPI, (b) Magenta indicates CQD cytoplasm and nuclei, (c) Overlay of (a) and (b) and (d) Brightfield image (scale: 20 μm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Viability of the RL-14 cells cultured with CQDs for 24 h. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Scaffold imaging using a multi-photon confocal microscope at variable depths. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Imaging cell nuclei stained with DAPI inside the 3D printed PLA scaffold at variable depths (a) outer layer, (b) z = 600 μm, (c) z = 800 μm, (d) z = 900 μm, (e) z = 1000 μm and (e) z = 1500 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Collagen derived carbon quantum dots for cell imaging in 3D scaffolds via two-photon spectroscopy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The University of Sydney, School of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, NSW 2006, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biocompatible and photostable fluorescent probes are crucial yet challenging to develop for visualising and tracking biological functions and interactions that occur in living organisms. We synthesised biocompatible CQDs with 15% QY and tested them for cellular and scaffold imaging at variable depths. The CQDs, synthesised from collagen under controlled and benign conditions in a hydrothermal reactor, were characterised for their key physicochemical properties. The fluorescence characteristics were determined using two-photon microscopy, and based on our results we propose a mechanism for CQD luminescence by combining the carbogenic core and edge-effect contributions to the PL behaviour. The bioimaging of cells embedded in a luminescent 3D printed scaffold showed that CQDs enable imaging at depths of about 1500 μm under biomimetic conditions. Real-time videography and imaging tests showed differential visualisation of individual cells and scaffold. The excellent photostability and non-photobleaching characteristics of CQDs make them suitable for long-term whole cell and tissue imaging via multi-photon microscopy.

ce:section.ce:para: Artificial organ transplant to reconstruct tissues that are lost or damaged by cancer, congenital anomaly or accidents has been a key focus of tissue engineering. A suitable solution would be to construct a support (scaffold) for cell growth that closely mimics the three-dimensional structure of the original tissue to assist in the proliferation and differentiation of the required cells [1]. Successful outcomes rely heavily on the design of the scaffold, which ideally should have an interconnected porous structure for cell seeding and eventual migration and growth in vascular channels. However, product consistency and quality control of scaffolds prior to implantation and acquiring of dynamic snapshots of tissue-host interactions with time remain major challenges.

Rapid prototyping (RP) to produce scaffolds on demand with specific design requirements, such as, mechanical properties, pore size and geometry have been receiving attention in biomedical applications due to its enormous flexibility and versatility [2]. For example, RP design enable design of scaffolds with optimum pore size distribution (between 100 and 500 μm) to transport the requisite nutrition into the microporous structure for cell survival [1]. Thus, RP enables fabrication of customised 3D structures having complex geometries with excellent reproducibility for use in implantology and regenerative medicine [2–4].

Current invasive imaging methods via histological examination and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) have intrinsic disadvantages such as lack of information on 3D structure and real-time dynamic transformations [5]. Multi-photon confocal microscopy is able to provide 3D information of optically non-transparent specimens at depths of several hundred microns using fluorescent probes. Over the past decade, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and synthetic dyes have been primarily used as two-photon probes. Despite high fluorescence, these materials suffer from rapid photobleaching and high toxicity due to the presence of heavy metals within the structure of semiconductor QDs [6]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are attractive alternatives as they offer high biocompatibility, photostability, and non-photobleaching and nontoxic characteristics [7]. Recent studies show that emissions from graphene quantum dots (GQDs) could be detected through a tissue phantom [8] and luminescent doped CQD hybrids with polymers (polyamide, polyurethane and polymethyl-methacrylate) are quite effective [9]. However, hybrids comprising CQD impregnated in biodegradable polymer scaffolds with precise control of size, configuration and structure is yet to be explored for biomedical applications.

In this work, non-invasive two-photon confocal microscopy was used to visualise the microstructure of luminescent scaffold design and cells deep inside the scaffold. We designed and synthesised two-photon-active CQDs using collagen as a precursor. The CQDs showed excellent colloidal stability, high photostability and negligible cytotoxicity. Luminescent scaffolds were constructed using a 3D printer in conjunction with poly (lactic acid) (PLA) filaments impregnated with CQDs. The 3D scaffold reconstruct was investigated for structural integrity and quality using microscopic imaging of the CQDs. Subsequently, cells seeded within microchannels in the scaffold were imaged at variable depths as shown in the schematic diagram of our setup (Fig. 1 ).

The Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, high glucose), fetal bovine serum (FBS) and trypsin were acquired from Gibco BRL (San Francisco,CA, USA). Collagen was derived from pig skin and PLA filaments were purchased from Bilby 3D systems (NSW, Australia). The PLA filaments had a density of 1.25 g/cm3, melting point of 200 °C and melt flow index (MFI) of 10 g/10 min. The RL-14 human fetal ventricular cardiomyocytes cells were obtained from Lonza (Allendale, NJ, USA). All chemical solvents and other media were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (NSW, Australia).

A JEOL 2100 transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV was used to obtain high-resolution TEM images. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of samples were recorded using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 spectrometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer microprobe using a monochromatic Al-K X-ray source (h = 1486.68 eV). PL emission measurements were performed using BMG LABTECH CLARIOstar Spectrometer. UV-Vis absorption was measured using a Cary 60 UV-Vis Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies). The PL life time decay profile was measured using a customised confocal microscope setup with 412 nm laser for excitation. The confocal microscopic images were obtained using a confocal multi-photon laser scanning microscope (Leica TCS SP II).

For CQD synthesis, 1.0 g collagen was dispersed in 20 mL deionised water at room temperature. This admixture was poured into a stainless steel autoclave with a teflon liner of 100 mL capacity and heated at 240 °C for 3 h. Subsequently, the reactor was cooled to room temperature. The dark brown mixture was collected via vacuum filtration and centrifuged twice at 6000 rpm for 30 min to yield a light yellow aqueous media with CQDs. The CQD containing solution was dialysed against ultrapure water using a tubular dialysis membrane (MWCO∼14 kDa) for 48 h and finally the purified mixture was concentrated to approximately 5 mg/mL for further characterisation. The yield was determined by freeze-drying method to be 38.0% of the precursor material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and cell viability experiments
ce:section.ce:para: RL-14 human fetal ventricular cardiomyocytes cells, were cultured in DMEM containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). The cell suspension was kept in an incubator (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA) at 37 °C, 5% CO2; and 95% humidity. Cell viability was measured using an automated TC10 cell counter. Briefly, cells were seeded in a 6 well plate in 1 mL cell culture media (DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS) at seeding density of 75 k cells/well. After overnight incubation, media was replaced with CQDs plus media for final concentrations ranging from 20 to 700 μg/mL cell culture media and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Media containing CQD was removed and cells were washed with sterile PBS. 10 μL of trypsinised cells were mixed with the trypen blue dye at 1:1 ratio and inserted into the counting slides. Untreated cells were used as control to calculate percent viability.

Cellular fluorescence images were recorded using a Leica TCS SP II laser scanning confocal microscope. RL-14 cells were seeded into 35 mm culture plates at a density of 105 cells/well in DMEM containing 10% FBS and incubated at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator for 24 h. After the removal of DMEM, a mixture of CQDs (500 μg/mL) in DMEM was added to each well for 24 h of incubation. Finally, the cells were washed twice with phosphate buffer solution to remove extracellular CQDs and were subsequently fixed with ethanol.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY, ϕ) of the CQDs was measured by a standard comparison method with respect to a reference. Quinine sulfate with a known QY of 0.5421 was introduced as a reference sample, and the main samples containing CQDs were diluted with DI water with variable concentrations. For each sample, the absorbance at the excitation wavelength (320 nm) was recorded. Then, the PL spectrum of the solution was measured and the integrated PL intensity was calculated. The procedures were repeated at varied concentrations and the integrated PL intensity versus absorbance data were analysed using statistical methods.

The fluorescence QY value was estimated using Eq (1), where ϕ is the QY, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical density. The subscripts “st” and “x” refer values that correspond to the standard with known QY and for the sample, respectively. The excitation/emission slit parameters were kept constant throughout the course of the PL measurements.
(1)
ϕ x
=
ϕ
s t
(
I x
/
I
s t
)
(
η x 2
/
η
s t
2
)
(
A
s t
/
A x
)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent scaffold 3D printing and cell seeding
ce:section.ce:para: The PLA filament was immersed in a solution of 1 mg/mL of CQDs for 24 h and subjected to drying in a vacuum desiccator for 24 h to achieve an even CQD dispersion on the filament. A cube of 5 mm dimension on each side (300 μm layer thickness) with a channel of 500 μm diameter at a distance of 1000 μm from the bottom of the cube (z = 0), was designed using a CAD software and fabricated using the printer (MakerBot Replicator Plus, USA) as shown in the schematic (Fig. 1). An inverted two-photon microscope with a 25× working objective lens (0.45 NA) was used to image the luminescent scaffold at various depths by changing the z axis from the bottom (z = 0) to 1000 and finally to 1500 μm (Δz = 1500 μm). Next, we seeded the RL-14 cells inside the scaffold. Prior to seeding, the scaffold was immersed in a solution of collagen in PBS (100 μg/mL) overnight. Cells were seeded at a density of 5 × 105 cells/scaffold and left in an incubator for 24 h for the cells to adhere to the PLA surface. The cells were fixed using an 80% EtOH solution and stained with 4',6-Diamidino-2-Phenylindole, Dihydrochloride (DAPI) for further characterisation.

The CQDs, prepared by our one-pot hydrothermal reaction (240 °C, 3 h residence time), had a quantum yield of 15%, which is higher than those for previously reported CQDs from natural sources [7,10,11]. A schematic illustration of the synthesis step and our imaging setup is shown in Fig. 1. The TEM images and particle size distribution of the CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 . The as-prepared CQDs show good dispersion in water, uniformity in size and spherical configuration. The particle size follows a Gaussian distribution with the most probable size of 1.25 ± 0.21 nm.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. S1 ESI) show the significant presence of functional groups on the surface of the CQDs. The stretching vibration band of CO at 1640 cm−1, and the stretching vibration bands of C-O at 1100 and 963 cm−1 indicate the presence of carboxylic acid and other oxygen-containing functional groups [12]. Furthermore, the absorption peak at 3080 cm−1 and one sharp peak at 1440 cm−1 are assigned to the vibration and deformation bands of N-H. In addition, two peaks at 2640 and 699 cm−1 ascribed to stretching vibration band of S-H and S-C, respectively, indicate the sulphur-containing functional groups. Moreover, three obvious absorption peaks at 2940, 1390 and 1300 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibration of C-H, CC and C-C, suggesting the presence of alkyl and aryl groups [13]. Raman spectra (Fig. S2 ESI) shows both the G band (related to in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon at 1590 cm−1) and the D band (related to the presence of sp3 defects is at 1320 cm−1) [14]. The broadness of the peaks suggests that CQDs consist of an amorphous core with predominantly sp2 carbon.

XPS measurements were performed to characterise the chemical composition of CQDs (Fig. 3 ). The three dominant peaks at ∼285.1 eV (C1s), ∼400.1 eV (N1s) and ∼531.2 eV (O1s) were observed in the full XPS survey spectrum. The high resolution C1s spectra are shown in Fig. 3b. The C1s envelope can be split into four Gaussian peaks. The peak centred at 284.6 is assigned to the graphitic sp2 C (C-C/CC) and the peaks centred at 286.0, 287.8 represent sp3 C (C-O, C-N), and carbonyl C (CO). The peak centred at and 288.7 eV is attributed to the carboxylate C(O)-O. The percentage areas of the deconvoluted N1s peaks exhibited a high proportion of pyrollic and pyridinic-N. The presence of a high percentage of pyridinic-N contributes one electron to the π system across the C atoms, known as a delocalised electron, which increases the probability of electron excitation for the same excitation energy. In contrast, pyrrolic-N (pentagonal structure with sp3 hybridisation) causes a high non-radiative transition [15].

The purified CQDs exhibited strong blue emission under UV light (365 nm), as well as blue, green and red colours during excitation with 370, 490 and 550 nm lasers, respectively (Fig. S3 ESI). The CQDs also presented interesting up-conversion along with down-conversion PL properties simultaneously during our experiments. The UV-Vis absorption, PL emission and fluorescence confocal microscopic images were recorded to characterise the details of CQD optical behaviour.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the CQDs showed two absorption peaks in the UV region at around 265 and ∼320 nm with a tail extending to the visible range. The absorption band at 265 nm is attributed to π→ π* transitions of the CC of sp2 C domains present in the sp3 matrix. The absorption band at ∼320 nm can be assigned to both the n → π* transition of the CO but also the transition of the conjugated C-N/CN bonds [16]. As mentioned in the high resolution N1s XPS analysis, the presence of conjugated pyrrole or pyrrolidone groups in conjunction with transitions of the CO groups are responsible for the absorption at ∼320 nm. This absorption band may be attributed to the transition from the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) of these groups [16].

The CQDs demonstrated excitation dependent down-conversion PL emission spectra (Fig. 4b), which were shifted to longer wavelengths with gradual decrease in intensity as a result of increase in the excitation wavelength. The strongest emission peak was shifted from 435 to 553 nm with a decrease in intensity as the excitation wavelength changed from 350 to 510 nm. Similar observations were reported by others for luminescent carbon nanomaterials, and are caused by the optical selection of different surface defect states near the Fermi level of CQDs [17,18]. To further investigate the dependence of PL behaviour on the chemical environment, qualitative relationship between pH values and emission was measured. The maximum intensity of the emission spectra was found to decrease under highly acidic or basic environment (Fig. 4c). The reason for this phenomenon is that large populations of H+ or OH− ions induce changes in the functional groups, and consequently the electronic transitions of the defects are disrupted or even prohibited [17]. Meanwhile, no bleaching was observed after constant irradiation with a 780 nm laser for 80 min (Fig. S4 ESI). Time-resolved fluorescence decay curve measured by time-correlated single photon counting method is shown in Fig. 4d. The decay curve was fitted satisfactorily to a double-exponential function with lifetimes of τ1 = 5.44 and τ2 = 1.44 ns. Multiexponential decay of PL emission is a characteristic feature of CQDs, and the decay originates from two discrete states at the bottom of the conduction band [19]. Crucially, in addition to the down-converted PL properties, the CQDs exhibit distinct up-converted characteristics. Fig. 5 shows the PL spectra of CQDs, excited by pulsed laser with wavelengths ranging from 720 to 880 nm with the maximum emission peak at 510 nm (780 nm excitation). The up-converted emission of CQDs stems from the multi-photon active process, in which the simultaneous absorption of two or more photons leads to the emission of light at a shorter wavelength than that of the excitation. The results show that the up-converted emission wavelength of CQDs can be finely tuned by adjusting the excitation wavelength.

Based on our results, we conclude that the two distinct peaks of the UV-Vis spectrum that are also noticeable in both the up- and down-conversion spectra may be the reason for the two distinct lifetime values. The two emission centres of the CQD (originating from the carbogenic core and surface defects) result in two distinct transitions with different energy levels (π→π* and n→π*), which in turn are manifest as twin peaks in the up- and down-conversion spectra [20]. The first peak with higher intensity is ascribed to the carbogenic core of the CQD, and the second peak with lower intensity (in both up- and down-conversion spectra) stems from the surface state, specifically from the delocalised electrons of pyridinic N. The XPS data show that the available surface states, namely the NC conjugated bonds are responsible for the n→π* transition. However, due to the presence of many non-radiative recombination processes (pyrrolic N and carboxylic groups) the latter asymmetric peak appears at a lower intensity [21].

To evaluate the potential of using the highly luminescent CQDs as optical labels for in vitro cell imaging, RL-14 (human fetal ventricular cardiomyocytes) cells were cultured in the presence of the synthesised CQDs. As depicted in Fig. 6 , strong fluorescencecan be seen after incubation with CQDs for 24 h. To eliminate autofluorescence and out of focus signals, the imaging experiments were conducted using a confocal microscope equipped with a pulsed laser operating at 780 nm. Fluorescence imaging at longer wavelengths, where tissue autofluorescence is essentially eliminated, allowed us to investigate the potential of CQDs as luminescent probes for cells without causing photo-damage to cells [6]. As shown in Fig. 6a, the CQDs penetrated into the cytoplasm and to a lesser extent into the nucleus of the RL-14 cells and exhibited bright fluorescence when excited at 780 nm. To unequivocally confirm the location of CQDs in the cells, we performed z-axis imaging of cells (Figure S5 ESI). With images spanning across the z-axis from the bottom (2 μm) to the top (12 μm, Δz = 2 μm) surface, we can clearly observe the fluorescent dots within the cell cytoplasm along the z-axis. These confirm the uptake of CQDs by cells and the dots are clearly located inside the cytoplasm rather than surficially bound or adsorbed on the RL-14 cells.

Cell viability experiments were conducted to evaluate the biocompatibility of the CQDs (Fig. 7 ). After cultivating the cells for 24 h with variable CQD concentrations, we observed that more than 95% of the cells were alive at concentrations as high as 500 μg/mL. These results are comparable to or even higher than those reported elsewhere for similar tests (Table S1) [7,17,22,23]. Our findings indicate that collagen derived CQDs possess excellent biocompatibility and are suitable for cell imaging and biological labelling.

The need to understand native tissue behaviour on incorporating cells into scaffolds and the ability to manufacture scaffolds with bespoke design specifications is of utmost importance in tissue engineering. An important parameter affecting cell response is the scaffold geometry including pore size, shape, strut size and orientation [2]. Scaffold architectures not only affect their mechanical performance but also affect permeability, nutrient diffusion and cell response [24]. The incorporation of CQDs enabled us tovisualise the tridimensional structure of the scaffold at various depths (from z = 0–1500 μm). The empty space (channel) can be observed in Fig. 8 a–e and the layers of the 3D printed scaffold at different z values can be visualised in real-time (video file 1, ESI). Our results show that the CQDs were able to absorb 780 nm laser (35 mW) at a depth of 1500 μm and emit strong enough signals to pass through the scaffold again and be detected by the photomultiplier tubes (PMTs). The total travel distance of light was measured to be 3000 μm in the PLA dense block. No return signal was detected by the PMTs in the absence of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 8f–j the scaffold can be visually profiled in detail at variable depths from zero to 1500 μm. The differential layers of the printed PLA block can be identified separately within 300 μm thickness. The strut sizes at high z values reported here are useful for the design, fabrication and quality control of the scaffold.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.02.006.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
video 1 Video 1

Identical imaging parameters were used to excite and visualise the cells (Fig. 9 ). The cell nuclei stained with DAPI can be clearly identified at z = 800 μm to z = 1500 μm. Due to the porous structure of the scaffold, some of the cells had sedimented onto the lower printed layers (z = 800 μm). On increasing the z value to 900, 1000 and 1500 μm, additional cells were detected (video file 2, ESI). We contend that such detailed cellular data from a bespoke scaffold and the ability to identify cell structures within the scaffold will enable the fabrication of customised scaffolds efficiently to investigate its interactions with tissues and track scaffold degradation on implantation.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.02.006.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Video 2 Video 2

We present a green and robust approach to synthesise high quality fluorescent CQDs by hydrothermal treatment of collagen without any additive or complex post-treatment processes. The CQDs exhibit up-conversion properties and prove to be efficient two-photon fluorescent probes for cellular and scaffold imaging. The combination of collagen based biocompatible CQDs and 3D printed scaffolds enabled accurate visualisation into the depths of our fabricated PLA scaffold using two-photon microscopy. Further, the CQDs displayed no photobleaching under repeated femtosecond NIR laser irradiation and were able to emit strong fluorescence over a wide range of pH values. It is anticipated that the significant imaging depths achieved with CQDs combined with their excellent biocompatibility and long-term photostability will enable advancements in implant design and visualisation in conjunction with two-photon imaging. The accurate non-invasive and stable monitoring of biological activities of cells within scaffolds, their interactions with tissues and the degradation of scaffolds will enable effective tissue engineering and reduce implant failure in regenerative medicine.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support through Australian Postgraduate Award (SC0042) (authors AD and SMA) and assistance from Dr D. Lai and Dr. S. Hua for the biology-related work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

ESI ESI

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI) available: Experimental details, FTIR and Raman spectra of the CQDs, Change in emission colour under different excitation wavelengths, PL stability and the cytotoxicity of the CQDs.
Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.02.006.
